title: Washington Gatekeeps AI Models While Beijing Considers Locking Down Its Own
subhead: GPT-5.6 clears a 12-day government review, Chinese models hit 46% of US enterprise AI traffic, and DeepSeek builds its own chip — the two sides of the AI sovereignty coin are hardening in real time.

sections.heading: GPT-5.6 Released After 12-Day Government Gate — Voluntary in Name Only
sections.body:
OpenAI's GPT-5.6 family — Sol, Terra, and Luna — became broadly available on July 9 after spending 12 days gated behind a US government review that was legally voluntary but functionally operated as preclearance. The review, conducted under President Trump's June 2 executive order on AI, involved Commerce Department testing by the Center for AI Standards and Innovation (CAISI) and direct engagement between OpenAI engineers and government officials in Washington.

For nearly two weeks, GPT-5.6 was accessible only to roughly 20 vetted organizations whose identities OpenAI had shared with the government — the first time a US AI lab gated a frontier model behind a state-approved customer list. Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick sought assurances from Sam Altman that all relevant federal agencies had completed their review before broader release. A White House spokesperson insisted the administration did not give OpenAI a 'green light, approval or clearance,' but the sequence — government requests restricted launch, government reviews model, government indicates no objection, company proceeds — tells a different story.

This follows the precedent set by Anthropic in June, when Commerce issued an export control directive under ECRA ordering Anthropic to disable Fable 5 and Mythos 5 for all foreign nationals. Anthropic pulled both models globally for 19 days before deploying a retrained classifier that blocks the flagged jailbreak in over 99% of attempts. The models returned on July 1.

Two different legal instruments — one mandatory directive, one 'voluntary' request — produced nearly identical outcomes: the government inserted itself into the frontier model release process and determined who could access it and when.
sections.whyItMatters: The US has effectively created a frontier AI release gate without passing any legislation to authorize one. The June 2 executive order explicitly states it does not authorize mandatory licensing, but the Anthropic export control and the GPT-5.6 review show that mandatory and voluntary pathways converge on the same result — government control over who gets access to the most capable AI systems and when.

sections.heading: Chinese AI Models Capture 46% of US Enterprise Traffic as Beijing Considers Export Controls
sections.body:
Chinese-origin AI models have surged to 46% of US enterprise token usage on OpenRouter, according to data reported by CNBC and International Business Times, up from an 11% average over the prior twelve months and just 4.5% in the first half of 2025. DeepSeek alone commands 17.6% of routed tokens (5.13 trillion weekly), followed by Alibaba's Qwen at 13.9% (2.77 trillion). Overall, Chinese-origin models now represent 46.4% of OpenRouter traffic, outpacing US-origin models at 35.7%.

The driver is cost: DeepSeek V4 Flash costs $0.14 per million input tokens compared to $5.00 for OpenAI's GPT-5.5 — a 35x price gap. Companies like Coinbase, Shopify, and Airbnb have publicly used or experimented with Chinese models.

The trend has triggered a joint investigation by the House Committee on Homeland Security and the House Select Committee on China. Rep. Andrew Garbarino, chairman of the Homeland Security Committee, told CNBC that 'the Chinese Communist Party is no longer just nipping at our heels in AI — it is racing to close the gap.' Meanwhile, Beijing is reportedly considering its own restrictions: China's Ministry of Commerce discussed measures to restrict overseas access to its most advanced AI models, according to a Reuters report, with meetings in May involving Alibaba, ByteDance, and Z.ai discussing a tiered regulatory framework that would keep frontier models under strict controls while leaving basic open-weight models largely accessible.

Analysts see the Chinese discussions as partly a response to Washington's AI controls. Rui Ma of TechBuzz China described it as 'policy parity,' while Kyle Chan of the Brookings Institution noted that 'if the US increasingly treats frontier AI models like export-controlled assets, China may feel it should have the same policy tools.'
sections.whyItMatters: A reciprocal lockdown is emerging. The US is restricting foreign access to frontier models; China is considering doing the same. Between them, enterprises worldwide face the prospect of both sides' most capable AI becoming harder to access — while the cheapest Chinese models fill the gap Washington's controls are creating.

sections.heading: DeepSeek Builds Its Own AI Chip as US Entity List Stalls
sections.body:
Chinese AI startup DeepSeek is developing its own AI chip, according to three people familiar with the matter reported by Reuters on July 7. The move is a direct response to US export controls that have restricted Chinese companies' access to Nvidia's most advanced GPUs — if you can't buy the hardware, build your own.

The chip effort runs alongside a striking political development: the US has not added any companies to its Entity List since October 2025, the longest gap in more than a decade. An interagency committee approved DeepSeek, memory chipmaker CXMT, and more than 100 other Chinese companies for addition to the blacklist last year — but the Commerce Department has never published those additions. Reuters reports that Under Secretary Jeffrey Kessler has sought to avoid listing Chinese parties for fear of escalating US-China tensions.

At least 75 Chinese entities in advanced semiconductor production, manufacturing equipment, and AI modeling were approved for listing but never published. Companies flagged for selling restricted Nvidia chips to Chinese universities, supplying Russian drones recovered in Poland, and producing military drones and robot dogs for China's armed forces all remain off the list.

Former Commerce official Kevin Kurland told Reuters that 'trade policy is overshadowing the use of a critical national security tool.' CSIS analyst Philip Luck compared the Entity List to 'whack-a-mole' and warned that the listing gap is 'likely allowing American technology to reach adversaries.'
sections.whyItMatters: The US has a growing implementation gap between its stated tech security policy and what it actually enforces. Over 100 approved blacklist entries sit unpublished while DeepSeek — the company that triggered Washington's AI alarm — pursues chip self-sufficiency. The longer the listing gap persists, the more it undermines the credibility of export controls as a tool.

sections.heading: South Korea First to Codify AI Red Teaming Standards
sections.body:
South Korea's Ministry of Science and ICT released two formal guidelines on July 8 — the AI Security Threat Response Manual and the AI Security Red Teaming Guide — establishing the world's first government-defined, auditable standard for how organizations must identify AI-specific security threats and structure adversarial testing teams. The release was timed to South Korea's 15th Information Protection Day Ceremony at the Shilla Hotel in Seoul, with presentations from CISA, the UK AI Safety Institute, and Google.

The guidelines directly address what the Georgetown Center for Security and Emerging Technology called the central obstacle to meaningful AI security: the complete absence of any government-defined standard means that an organization's claim to have red-teamed its AI systems has been impossible to independently verify. South Korea's framework creates a compliance baseline that can be audited against defined criteria, covering adversarial inputs, model poisoning, inference attacks, and AI-assisted cyberattacks.

The move is backed by South Korea's AI Basic Act, which took effect January 22, 2026, creating legally binding obligations for developers and operators of AI systems — including foreign companies whose products affect Korean users. The framework is the first anywhere to treat AI red-teaming not as a voluntary best practice but as a regulatory requirement with defined standards and auditability.
sections.whyItMatters: South Korea has moved from voluntary norms to enforceable standards. With the EU AI Act still in its early implementation and the US relying on executive orders rather than legislation, Seoul has become the first jurisdiction where 'we red-teamed our model' is a claim that regulators can actually verify. Other governments — including CISA and the UK AI Safety Institute, which attended the launch — are watching closely.

sections.heading: Singapore's AI-Driven Export Surge and Temasek's $400 Billion Bet on Sovereign AI
sections.body:
Singapore's non-oil domestic exports jumped 38.4% year-on-year in May, fueled primarily by AI-related shipments, according to Reuters data cited in global headlines. The surge signals sustained Asian tech supply chain momentum even amid global trade uncertainty, and positions Singapore as a key node in the AI hardware and services corridor.

Separately, Singapore state investor Temasek reported a record net portfolio value of S$518 billion (US$400 billion), and announced it will target AI holdings at 15% of its portfolio by 2031, according to the company's president. Temasek is avoiding new crypto bets after its FTX losses, and is keeping 25% of its portfolio liquid — roughly $100 billion — to weather shocks and seize AI-related opportunities. The firm also named Palantir as a preferred partner for AI in regulated and sovereign markets through a Rackspace partnership.

The Rackspace-Palantir deal specifically targets what it calls 'sovereign markets' — jurisdictions where governments require AI compute and data to remain within national borders. It reflects a broader trend: as countries tighten AI governance, companies that can guarantee data residency and local compute are gaining a structural advantage in government and enterprise contracts.
sections.whyItMatters: Singapore is quietly becoming the AI infrastructure hub of Southeast Asia — not just through export volume, but through sovereign wealth deployment targeting 15% AI portfolio exposure. Temasek's strategy reflects the bet that 'sovereign AI' — compute and data that stays within national borders — will be the dominant procurement model for governments worldwide.

sections.heading: France Holds Digital Tax Line as Trump Threatens 100% Wine Tariffs at G7
sections.body:
French President Emmanuel Macron told reporters at the G7 summit in Evian that France will not drop its digital services tax on American tech giants, just hours before meeting Trump. The French digital services tax, which primarily targets US technology companies operating in France, has been a longstanding irritant in US-France trade relations.

Trump reportedly threatened a 100% tariff on French wines in response. The exchange reignites a trade conflict that predates the current administration — France first implemented its digital services tax in 2019, prompting Trump to threaten similar tariffs in his first term. The difference now is context: the G7 summit was meant to focus on the Iran deal and Ukraine, but digital taxation has reemerged as a persistent fault line.

The digital tax dispute illustrates a broader pattern: European governments are increasingly treating US tech platforms as fiscal targets, while Washington views those taxes as discriminatory against American companies. The EU has its own Digital Markets Act and is advancing AI Act implementation, creating a thickening web of tech regulation that US firms must navigate — and that US policymakers view as trade barriers in disguise.
sections.whyItMatters: The digital tax fight at the G7 is a reminder that tech geopolitics isn't just about chips and AI models — it's also about who gets to tax the platforms that dominate digital markets. France is holding the line on taxing US tech revenue, and Trump's 100% wine tariff threat shows how quickly digital policy disputes escalate into broader trade confrontations.

sections.heading: Türkiye Tech Angle: AI Sovereignty Moves East
sections.body:
At a Rio conference, Brazilian president Lula's top adviser Celso Amorim framed rare earths and data sovereignty as strategic priorities for the Global South, arguing that Latin America and Europe are 'bystanders in the US-China AI race' and must respond through BRICS cooperation. The framing is relevant to Turkey, which sits at a similar structural position — not a frontier AI developer, but a significant digital economy with growing compute demands.

Turkey's position in the AI sovereignty landscape is defined by its dependence on foreign AI infrastructure. With US models increasingly gated behind government review (Anthropic's export controls, OpenAI's customer-vetted rollout) and Chinese models potentially facing Beijing's own export restrictions, Turkish enterprises face a narrowing set of options for frontier AI access. The Temasek-Palantir-Rackspace 'sovereign markets' partnership model — where AI compute stays within national borders — could offer a template for Turkish data center investment.

The broader regional implication: as the US and China both move toward controlling who accesses their most capable AI, middle powers like Turkey, Brazil, and Gulf states have a growing incentive to build domestic compute capacity and negotiate bilateral access rather than rely on open global markets that are closing fast.
sections.whyItMatters: For Turkey, the dual lockdown — US models restricted by Washington, Chinese models potentially restricted by Beijing — means that the open global AI market is shrinking. Countries that don't build or secure their own compute infrastructure will find their access to frontier AI increasingly mediated by someone else's export controls.

bottomLine: Two parallel developments crystallized this week: the US government has effectively created a frontier AI release gate through executive action — first blocking Anthropic's models via export control, then clearing GPT-5.6 through a 'voluntary' 12-day review that functioned as preclearance. Meanwhile, Chinese AI models now handle nearly half of US enterprise AI traffic on OpenRouter, and Beijing is considering its own export controls on advanced models. DeepSeek is building its own chip, the US Entity List has had no new additions in eight months despite over 100 approved entries, and South Korea became the first country to make AI red-teaming an auditable legal standard. The AI sovereignty contest is no longer theoretical — both Washington and Beijing are actively shaping who gets access to frontier models, and enterprises worldwide are caught in between.
sources: TechTimes, Forbes, CNBC, Reuters, International Business Times, Analytics India Magazine, Firstpost, Memeburn, CSIS, Trend Micro, Channel News Asia, Yahoo Finance, SCMP headlines, Frontion News headlines
